coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of PVDF/TiO2@CQDs photocatalytic films by ink-jet printing for dye degradation under visible light
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Zihan
Qin, Shuhao
Yang, Jingkui
Wu, Xiao
Zhang, Jing
Li, Jianxin
Wang, Hong
Cui, Zhenyu
coredata.dc:description: PVDF/TiO2@CQDs hybrid photocatalytic thin films were prepared by ink-jet printing. The printing ink was prepared with the TiO2, TiO2(s)@CQDs (surface composite), and TiO2(i)@CQDs (situ doping) nanoparticles as precursors, respectively. Compared to TiO2(s)@CQDs, TiO2(i)@CQDs has a smaller particle size (about 10.01 nm) and a narrower particle size distribution (6∼14 nm), which can improve the photocatalytic effect while avoiding nozzle clogging. The PVDF/TiO2(i)@CQDs hybrid film exhibited a high degradation efficiency (η) of 93.71 % for Rhodamine B (RhB) within 150 min under visible light with a catalyst load of only 0.49 wt%. The reaction rate constant (ks) of the PVDF/TiO2(i)@CQDs film was about 4.3 times higher than that of the PVDF/TiO2(s)@CQDs film. The small particle size of this S-scheme TiO2(i)@CQDs heterostructure nanoparticle and the more effective contact interface formed between TiO2 and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) further facilitated electron transfer and enhanced photoactivity. The firmly immobilized catalysts on the Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) film substrate through Ti-F coordination bonds ensured the reusability and stability of the photocatalytic film (the η of RhB still reached 88.05 % after 10 consecutive cycles). This work provides a new strategy for the application of ink-jet printing technology in photocatalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ink-jet printing
TiO2@CQDs
Hybrid film
Photocatalytic degradation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the PVDF film substrate and CQDs
Fabrication of hybrid film by ink-jet printing
Optical properties and charge transfer of films

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Innovation Capacity Building Foundation of Scientific Research Institutions in Guizhou Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22178267 and 21978213) and the Innovation Capacity Building Foundation of Scientific Research Institutions in Guizhou Province ([2023]001). We would also like to thank the Analytical & Testing Center of Tiangong University for Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the fabrication of hybrid films by ink-jet printing.
(a) SEM image and (b, c) TEM images of the original TiO2. TEM images of (d–f) TiO2(s)@CQDs and (g–i) TiO2(i)CQDs.
(a) XRD patterns, (b) FTIR patterns, (c) XPS spectra, (d) Ti 2p and (e) O1s spectra.
(a, b) VB-XPS spectra, (c, d) UV–vis DRS spectra of TiO2 and CQDs, and (e) Schematic diagram of S-scheme TiO2@CQDs heterostructure.
Surface SEM images of (a) T1, (b) T15, and (c) T20. (d) EDS results of T15. (e) Cross-section SEM images and element mapping of T15. Surface SEM images of (f) T(s)C15 and (g) T(i)C15.
AFM images and roughness of (a) T15, (b) T(s)C15, and (c) T(i)C15.
(a) UV–vis DRS, (b) Band gaps, (c) PL spectra, (d) EIS spectra and (e) Schematic of charge transfer.
(a) The degradation curves of MB under UV light. (b) The degradation curves of RhB under visible light. (c) plots of ln(C/C 0) versus time (d) ks values.
(a, b) XPS spectra and (c) Cycling properties of T(i)C15.
(a) The degradation curves of RhB using T(i)C15 with various scavengers. (b) EPR spectra.
Photocatalytic mechanism of T(i)C15 under visible light.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of PVDF/TiO2@CQDs photocatalytic films by ink-jet printing for dye degradation under visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering/State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes/National Center for International Joint Research on Separation Membranes, Tiangong University, Tianjin 300387, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Computer Science and Technology, Tiangong University, Tianjin 300387, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Computer Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Computer Science and Technology, Tiangong University, Tianjin 300387, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guizhou Material Industry Technology Research Institute, Guiyang 550014, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guizhou Material Industry Technology Research Institute

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guizhou Material Industry Technology Research Institute, Guiyang 550014, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: PVDF/TiO2@CQDs hybrid photocatalytic thin films were prepared by ink-jet printing. The printing ink was prepared with the TiO2, TiO2(s)@CQDs (surface composite), and TiO2(i)@CQDs (situ doping) nanoparticles as precursors, respectively. Compared to TiO2(s)@CQDs, TiO2(i)@CQDs has a smaller particle size (about 10.01 nm) and a narrower particle size distribution (6∼14 nm), which can improve the photocatalytic effect while avoiding nozzle clogging. The PVDF/TiO2(i)@CQDs hybrid film exhibited a high degradation efficiency (η) of 93.71 % for Rhodamine B (RhB) within 150 min under visible light with a catalyst load of only 0.49 wt%. The reaction rate constant (ks) of the PVDF/TiO2(i)@CQDs film was about 4.3 times higher than that of the PVDF/TiO2(s)@CQDs film. The small particle size of this S-scheme TiO2(i)@CQDs heterostructure nanoparticle and the more effective contact interface formed between TiO2 and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) further facilitated electron transfer and enhanced photoactivity. The firmly immobilized catalysts on the Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) film substrate through Ti-F coordination bonds ensured the reusability and stability of the photocatalytic film (the η of RhB still reached 88.05 % after 10 consecutive cycles). This work provides a new strategy for the application of ink-jet printing technology in photocatalysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The deterioration of the water environment caused by the discharge of large quantities of industrial wastewater has become increasingly prominent with the acceleration of human urbanization and industrialization. Dye wastewater has drawn widespread attention due to its large discharge and treatment difficulty. As a clean advanced oxidation technology, photocatalysis exhibits great potential in the field of organic wastewater purification with its strong redox capacity and green sustainability [1]. Titanium dioxide (TiO2) exhibits good photocatalytic properties, stable physicochemical properties and non-toxicity. However, TiO2 has low visible light activity and can only absorb about 4 % of ultraviolet light from solar light, hindering its utilization for solar energy [2]. In addition, traditional TiO2 photocatalysts are usually applied to wastewater treatment as suspended powder, which is easily agglomerated, difficult to recycle, and causes secondary pollution of water bodies, thus limiting its photoactivity and large-scale application [3].

The visible light activity of TiO2 can be effectively improved by elemental doping [4], morphology control [5], and heterostructure construction [6]. Among them, the construction of TiO2-based heterostructures has attracted much attention because of its ability to broaden the photoresponse range while effectively inhibiting photogenerated electron-hole recombination, thus improving photocatalytic properties [7]. As an important member of the carbon nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are good candidates for the construction of TiO2-based heterostructures due to their superior electron transfer ability, quantum confinement effect, and ultrasmall size. Sui et al. [8], Sargin et al. [9], and Mendeley et al. [10] have prepared composites by coupling CQDs with TiO2, which improved the light absorption capacity and photocatalytic properties of TiO2. Nevertheless, the specific interfacial charge transfer mechanism between CQDs and TiO2 remains to be further clarified. In addition, a good and efficient heterostructure interface needs to be designed and constructed to facilitate electron transfer, thus enhancing photoactivity.

The preparation of hybrid catalytic films by immobilizing powder photocatalysts on film substrates is one of the most effective strategies for solving the above issues. Films are capable of providing carriers for photocatalysts while improving their dispersibility and utilization [11,12]. Coating techniques (such as scraping, dip coating, and spin coating) are currently the most common methods for preparing hybrid films, where catalysts are coated on the surface of the film to ensure that it is sufficiently exposed to light. Conventional coating techniques, such as scraping, suffer from the drawbacks of thick coatings, low precision, and inapplicability to large-scale preparation. The low utilization of coating precursors (<5%) and sensitivity to substrate defects limit the application of spin coating and dip coating [13]. Compared to traditional coating technologies, ink-jet printing exhibits great controllability and thinner, more uniform coatings with higher catalyst utilization. In addition, it can reach 98 % utilization of precursor (a small amount of waste is generated during the cleaning of the nozzle), effectively avoiding precursors waste [14]. Therefore, ink-jet printing has a promising future and is becoming a highly competitive alternative technology due to its speed, flexibility and suitability for large-area, batch production. The ink-jet printing technology has been applied to sensors [15], transistors [16], electrode materials [17], nanogenerators [18], and filter membrane modification [19]. However, until now, there have been few applications in photocatalysis.

In general, there are two approaches to prepare TiO2-based hybrid films by ink-jet printing [20]: One class uses tetrabutyl titanate (TBOT) sol as a precursor, and then the sol is prepared as an ink to be printed onto the film substrate and calcined at 300∼500 °C to obtain TiO2 coated films. Calcination plays a role in promoting the crystallization of precursors and improving the bonding between TiO2 and the substrate. The disadvantages of this method are the high temperature requirements of the substrate (not applicable to substrates such as polymers), the difficulty in adjusting the physical and chemical properties, and the tendency to introduce toxic agents. Another type of approach is to directly prepare TiO2 nanoparticles into printable ink. This method can be applied to many types of substrates without post-calcination treatment of the material, and the TiO2 crystal type can be determined before printing. However, the nozzle is easily clogged by TiO2 nanoparticles. Therefore, it is crucial to obtain ink with small TiO2 particle size, uniform distribution, and better dispersion for this printing method. Currently, the application of ink-jet printing in photocatalysis is rare and usually involves printing the catalyst onto a glass substrate and followed by heat treatment [21,22]. Arin et al. [23] investigated a chemical solution deposition method using Ti precursors for ink-jet printing on glass substrates. The printed materials were calcined at 500–650 °C to obtain TiO2 coatings. The hybrid materials demonstrated good photocatalytic degradation properties for methyl orange. Cerna et al. [24] synthesized TiO2 nanoparticles using a hydrothermal method and evaluated the photocatalytic properties of the materials by printing the prepared TiO2 ink on soda-lime glass calcined at 500 °C. However, the preparation of hybrid photocatalytic films by ink-jet printing still faces several issues: (1) The large particle size of commercial TiO2 (about 20 nm) leads to a high risk of nozzle clogging. (2) The bonding between TiO2 and the substrate cannot be ensured. High-temperature calcination is required after printing onto glass substrates to improve bonding but it does not apply to polymer substrates. (3) The low visible light activity of TiO2 still hinders the practical application of TiO2-coated photocatalytic films.

Here, hybrid photocatalytic films with visible light activity were prepared by depositing modified TiO2 photocatalysts on Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) films using an ink-jet printing method. First, TiO2, TiO2(s)@CQDs (surface composite) and TiO2(i)@CQDs (in situ doped) nanoparticles were prepared. The phase structure, morphology, particle size, size distribution, and specific surface area of the nanoparticles were characterized and compared. Then, each of the three nanoparticles was formulated as a printing ink and printed on the PVDF film substrate. The effects of the printing times on the morphology and photocatalytic properties of the hybrid films were studied, so that the optimal printing times were determined. The optical, charge transport, and separation performances of the films were investigated. The photocatalytic degradation of dyes under ultraviolet and visible light by hybrid film was evaluated, and the photocatalytic mechanism was proposed. In addition, the bonding between the catalyst and the polymer film substrate as well as the reusability properties of the photocatalytic film were explored. This work provides a solution for the preparation of stable polymer-based photocatalytic films by ink-jet printing technology. The obtained hybrid films have thin and uniform coatings, high catalyst utilization, and are suitable for batch production.

PVDF was provided by 3F New Materials Co. Ltd (China). Citric acid, urea, and sodiumhexametaphosphate ((NaPO3)6) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd (China). Methylene Blue (MB) and Rhodamine B (RhB) and ethanol (EtOH) were supplied by Tianjin Feng Chuan Chemical Reagents Factory (China). TiO2 (P25, 80 % anatase and 20 % rutile) was provided by Degussa (China). Tetrabutyl titanate (TBOT), N, N-dimethylacetamide (DMAC), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the PVDF film substrate and CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The PVDF film substrates and CQDs were obtained by the phase inversion method and the hydrothermal method, respectively. Details are provided in the Supplementary Material.

TiO2(s)@CQDs (surface composite) nanoparticles were synthesized by the hydrothermal method (Fig. 1 .). In detail, 1 g of TiO2 was dispersed in 100 g of ultrapure water and stirred for 45 min before adding 0.01 g of CQDs. The mixed system was stirred for 60 min and sonicated for 30 min. The mixture was then poured into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and reacted at 150 °C for 2 h. Finally, the mixture solution was centrifuged and washed to obtain TiO2(s)@CQDs.

TiO2(i)@CQDs (in situ doped) nanoparticles were synthesized by the “in situ hydrothermal-vapor induction” method (Fig. 1). First, 4.26 mL of TBOT was mixed with 20 mL of EtOH and stirred for 45 min. Subsequently, 0.01 g of CQDs were added and sonicated for 30 min. The mixture was poured into a 25 mL Teflon container. Then, the above container was put in a larger container (150 mL) with 6 mL of ultrapure water (ultrapure water was used to induce the crystallization of TiO2 to form anatase), which was subsequently placed in a stainless steel autoclave at 150 °C for 10 h to obtain TiO2(i)@CQDs.

The obtained nanoparticles were formulated as ink for ink-jet printing. First, 0.002 g of (NaPO3)6 dispersant was dissolved in 100 g of ultrapure water. Then, 1 g of nanoparticles (TiO2, TiO2(s)@CQDs or TiO2(i)@CQDs) was added. The pH was adjusted to 9 by adding an appropriate amount of NaOH, after which the ink was sonicated for 20 min to obtain a stable ink.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of hybrid film by ink-jet printing
ce:section.ce:para: The PVDF film substrate was flat on A4 paper and printed in a pre-set pattern using a commercial ink-jet printer (HP DJ 2723). The film was dried at 45 °C for 20 min after each print to ensure complete drying before the next print. The PVDF/TiO2, PVDF/TiO2(s)@CQDs and PVDF/TiO2(i)@CQDs hybrid films were prepared by inks formulated with TiO2, TiO2(s)@CQDs and TiO2(i)@CQDs nanoparticles, respectively. They were named TX (X = 1, 10, 15, 20 and 30, X represents the number of prints), T(s)C15 and T(i)C15, respectively. The number 15 represents 15 prints. The detailed preparation process is shown in Fig. 1.

The microstructure of the materials was investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HTEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectrocopy (FTIR), and X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS). The specific surface area of the samples was tested by a specific surface analyzer (BET, NOVA4200e). The optical performances were tested by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS). The charge separation efficiency was characterized by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and photoluminescence spectra (PL). Detailed test methods are described in the Supplementary Material.

The photocatalytic properties of the hybrid films (2 cm × 2 cm) under ultraviolet (UV) light and visible light were tested using a UV-lamp (λ = 365 nm) and a xenon lamp with a UV cut-off filter (λ > 420 nm), respectively. MB solution (20 mg/L) and RhB solution (20 mg/L) acted as the target degradant. Prior to the degradation experiment, the mixed solution with the placed hybrid film was stirred under darkness for 60 min to reach the equilibrium of adsorption/desorption. The details are given in the Supplementary Material.

Fig. 2 (a–c) shows the morphology of the original TiO2. The surface of the original TiO2 is relatively smooth (Fig. 2(a–b)). As shown in the HTEM image (Fig. 2(c)), the lattice spacing of 0.352 nm corresponds to the (101) face of the anatase TiO2 [25]. As shown in the TEM images of TiO2(s)@CQDs (Fig. 2(d–e)), the CQDs composite on the TiO2 surface to form the TiO2(s)@CQDs heterostructure nanoparticles, in which the average particle size of TiO2 is about 21.46 nm, and the size distribution range is wide (5∼40 nm). The average particle size of the CQDs was small (∼2.91 nm, Fig. S1). Furthermore, the particle size of the TiO2(s)@CQDs heterostructure nanoparticles increased slightly due to the CQDs composite on the TiO2 surface. As shown in the HTEM image of TiO2(s)@CQDs (Fig. 2(f)), the 0.273 nm lattice spacing corresponds to the (002) crystal surface of CQDs [26]. The average particle size of TiO2(i)@CQDs nanoparticles significantly reduced (∼10.01 nm), with a narrower particle size distribution (6∼14 nm) (Fig. 2(g)). The CQDs were embedded within the TiO2 (Fig. 2(h)) and did not cause an increase in the TiO2(i)@CQDs particle size. The smaller size and narrower size distribution of TiO2(i)@CQDs enhanced the photocatalytic efficacy while reducing the risk of nozzle clogging, thus increasing its applicability to ink-jet printing technology. Additionally, two lattices with different orientations agglomerated at the junction, suggested the construction of TiO2@CQDs heterostructure (Fig. 2(i)). Moreover, a more effective contact interface was established between the CQDs and TiO2, which can enhance the charge transport performance of the heterostructures.

The specific surface area of the nanoparticles was explored by N2 adsorption–desorption method (Fig. 3 .). The calculated specific surface areas by BET of TiO2, TiO2(s)@CQDs and TiO2(i)@CQDs were 55.652 m2 g−1, 59.694 m2 g−1 and 144.281 m2 g−1, respectively. Compared to TiO2, the specific surface area of TiO2(s)@CQDs slightly increased, which can be attributed to the large specific surface area produced by the small-sized CQDs on the TiO2 surface. The much smaller size of TiO2(i)@CQDs leads to a significant increase in specific surface area, which can promote the reaction between the catalyst and the target pollutants, and thus improve the catalytic efficacy.

The phase structure of the nanoparticles was detected by XRD (Fig. 4 (a)). As shown in the XRD results of original TiO2 and TiO2(s)@CQDs, the peaks at 25.3°, 37.8°, 48.0°, 53.9° and 55.2° correspond to anatase TiO2 (JCPDS 21–1272), while the peaks at 27.5°, 36.1° and 41.3° are the characteristic peaks of rutile TiO2 (JCPDS 21–1276) [27]. Therefore, the crystalline types of original TiO2 and TiO2(s)@CQDs are mainly anatase mixed with a small amount of rutile. The TiO2 in TiO2(i)@CQDs prepared by the “in situ hydrothermal-vapor induction” method exhibits good crystallization properties with anatase phase. Furthermore, according to the Debye-Scherrer equation (D = Kλ/βcosθ) (D represents the crystalline size, K represents a constant, λ refers to the wavelength of irradiation, β represents the full-width half maximum of the (101) surface obtained by the jade software, and θ refers to the Bragg diffraction angle), the calculated crystallite sizes of TiO2 in TiO2(s)@CQDs and TiO2(i)@CQDs are about 20.58 nm and 9.67 nm, respectively, which are inconsistent with the TEM results.

The broad absorption peak around 3000∼3500 cm−1 and the peak at 1630 cm−1 of the original TiO2 correspond to the O–H and –OH stretching vibrations, respectively [28]. These two characteristic peaks of TiO2(s)@CQDs and TiO2(i)@CQDs are significantly enhanced compared to the original TiO2, which is ascribed to oxygen-containing groups of CQDs (such as –OH and –COOH). It further confirms the construction of the TiO2@CQDs heterostructure.

The XPS results of the nanoparticles are shown in Fig. 4(c–e). The full survey scan of TiO2 (Fig. 4(c)) exhibits three primary characteristic peaks of C, Ti, and O, where the C element comes from the adventitious C [29]. The trace amounts of N elemental in TiO2(s)@CQDs and TiO2(i)@CQDs are derived from CQDs [30]. As shown in the Ti 2p spectra (Fig. 4(d)), the two strong peaks observed near 458.06 eV and 464.4 eV for the original TiO2 assigned to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, respectively [31]. In contrast, Ti 2p peaks in TiO2(s)@CQDs and TiO2(i)@CQDs shifted to high binding energies, suggesting that strong electronic interactions exist at the heterogeneous interface of TiO2 with CQDs [26,32]. The O 1 s XPS spectra were divided into three single peaks representing the C = O/C-OH (532.46 eV), O–H (532.28 eV) and Ti-O (530.75 eV), respectively (Fig. 4(e)). The enhancement of C = O/C-OH and O–H peaks in TiO2(s)@CQDs and TiO2(i)@CQDs further demonstrated that the heterostructures inherited the oxygen-containing groups from CQDs, such as C = O and –OH.

The energy band structures of TiO2 and CQDs and the heterostructure types of TiO2@CQDs were thoroughly investigated. As shown in the UV–vis DRS plot (Fig. 5 (a, b)), the original TiO2 only absorbs ultraviolet light below 420 nm, while CQDs exhibit strong light harvesting ability for visible light (400 ∼ 700 nm). The Tacus plots (insets of Fig. 5(a, b)) show that the band gaps (Eg) are 3.15 eV and 2.81 eV for TiO2 and CQDs, respectively. In addition, as shown in the VB-XPS results (Fig. 5(c, d)), the values of the valence bands (EVB-XPS ) of TiO2 and CQDs are 3.11 eV and 1.91 eV, respectively. The EVB (EVB vs. NHE) of the corresponding standard hydrogen electrode can be obtained by the formula:
E
VB
v s . N H E
=
w f
+ E
VB - X P S
- 4.44
, in which wf (4.2 eV) refers to the work function of the XPS instrument [33]. The calculated EVB for TiO2 and CQDs are 2.87 eV and 1.67 eV, respectively. According to the formula:
Eg = E
VB
-
E
CB
, the ECB of TiO2 and CQDs are −0.28 eV and −1.14 eV, respectively. In addition, ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) was used to characterize the work functions (Φ) of TiO2 and CQDs (Fig. S2). Φ was calculated using the equation [34]:
Φ = h v -
E
cutoff
,
where hv and Ecutoff are the incoming photon energy from the He I source (21.21 eV) and the secondary electron cutoff energy, respectively. The calculated Φ of TiO2 and CQDs were 7.31 eV and 6.29 eV. TiO2 has a large Φ and a lower Fermi energy level (EF ), while CQDs exhibit a smaller Φ and a higher EF . Based on the above data, the TiO2@CQDs heterostructure is consistent with the S-scheme heterostructure [35]. The schematic diagram of the TiO2@CQDs heterostructure is shown in Fig. 5(e). As TiO2 and CQDs are in close contact, the free electrons in TiO2 transfer to the CQDs until an equilibrium EF is reached. This charge redistribution effect at the interface results in the establishment of a build-in electric field (IEF) between TiO2 and CQDs. Furthermore, the band edges of TiO2 bend downward due to electrons aggregation, while the band edges of CQDs bend upward. Under light irradiation, photogenerated electrons (e-) were driven by IEF and band bending to transfer from the conduction band (CB) of TiO2 to the valence band (VB) of CQDs and to bind with photogenerated holes (h+), which accelerated charge transfer and enhanced photoactivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and charge transfer of films
ce:section.ce:para: The light absorption capacity of the hybrid films was tested by UV–vis DRS (Fig. 8 (a)). Compared to T15, the absorption capacity of visible light is enhanced in both T(s)C15 and T(i)C15 coupled with CQDs. Eg can be obtained by the Tauc plots (ahv vs hv), where a is the absorption coefficient and hv represents the energy. The Eg of T15, T(s)C15, and T(i)C15 are 3.15 eV, 3.02 eV, and 2.91 eV, respectively (Fig. 8(b)). As depicted in the PL spectra (Fig. 8(c)), compared to T15, the charge recombination efficiencies of T(s)C15 and T(i)C15 are significantly reduced with the introduction of CQDs. Furthermore, T(i)C15 exhibits a lower charge recombination efficiency than T(s)C15, indicating its superior charge transfer capability and the electron-hole recombination is suppressed. The Nyquist plot (Fig. 8(d)) also shows the smallest arc radius for T(i)C15, further indicating the smallest electron transport resistance and the fastest electron migration efficiency.

The excellent visible light activity, charge separation, and transport properties of T(i)C15 can be attributed to the following: On the one hand, Eg is reduced by the chemical and electronic coupling between the conduction band state of TiO2 and the graphite intrinsic π-state of CQDs, which enhances the absorption ability of the material to visible light [36]. On the other hand, the superior electron-absorbing ability of CQDs endows the S-scheme TiO2@CQDs heterostructure with excellent electron transport properties and suppresses electron-hole recombination. Moreover, the CQDs of TiO2(i)@CQDs are embedded interior of the TiO2, which creates a larger and more efficient charge transfer interface between the CQDs and TiO2, further accelerating the charge transfer. The schematic of charge transfer for the three photocatalysts is illustrated in Fig. 8(e).

The photocatalytic degradation ability of the films for MB under UV light was tested and the effect of the printing number on the photocatalytic performance was explored. As shown in Fig. 9 (a), η first increases gradually with the printing number, and the degradation efficiency (η) reaches the maximum when the printing number is 15 (T15) (η of 96.84 % for MB within 210 min), which is attributed to the fact that TiO2 reaches a uniform and dense distribution on the film surface. The decrease of η with a further increase in the number of prints may be due to the dense stacking of photocatalysts on the film surface, which reduced the specific surface area of the nano-photocatalysts and decreased the contact with pollutants, thus hindering the photoactivity. In addition, the photocatalytic ability of films under visible light was explored (Fig. 9(b)). T15 hardly degraded RhB under visible light, while T(s)C15 showed good visible photocatalytic performance. T(i)C15 exhibited the highest photocatalytic degradation performance, with a η of 93.71 % for RhB within 150 min. The apparent rate constants (k) of T(s)C15 and T(i)C15 are 0.0042 min−1 and 0.0182 min−1, respectively (Fig. 9(c)). For film materials, the reaction rate constant (ks) can be obtained by the following equation [37]:
k = k s
A V
, where A (cm2) and V (mL) represent the effective film area and the reaction solution volume, respectively. The ks can be used to evaluate the photocatalytic capacity per unit film area at a certain light intensity and initial dye concentration. The ks value of T(i)C15 was calculated according to k to be 0.1138 mL min−1 cm−2, which is more than 4.3 times that of T(s)C15 (0.0263 mL min−1 cm−2) (Fig. 9(d)). Compared to the photocatalytic films prepared in the literature using the ink-jet printing technique, the photocatalytic films prepared in this work exhibited a superior photocatalytic degradation performance (Table 1 ).

The bonding between the photocatalyst and the film substrate is crucial for the stability of the material in practical application. The bonding between the photocatalyst and the PVDF film was explored by XPS (Fig. 10 (a, b)), a single F 1 s peak of the original PVDF film is due to the single chemical environment of F atoms in PVDF. On the contrary, two distinct peaks were detected in the F 1 s spectra of T(i)C15, indicating the Ti-F coordination bond was formed between the photocatalysts and PVDF film [43,44]. In addition, the C-F peak of T(i)C15 in the FTIR spectra shifted to a lower wavenumber compared to original PVDF film, further demonstrating the formation of the Ti-F coordination bond (Fig. S5). The Ti-F coordination bond ensures the firmly immobilization of the catalyst on the PVDF film substrate and enhances the stability of the hybrid film in use. The reusability of the hybrid film was studied by multi-cycle degradation experiments (Fig. 10(c)), the η of the film for RhB could still remain at 88.05 % after 10 consecutive cycles. Additionally, XPS and SEM tests were performed on T(i)C15 before and after recycling (Fig. S6). After 10 cycles, the chemical composition and morphology of the film remained almost unchanged. This indicates that the catalysts still firmly immobilized on the film surface. These results indicate that the hybrid film prepared by ink-jet printing method with PVDF film as carriers has better stability and reusability and exhibits great potential for practical water treatment.

Free radicals were realized by scavenging experiments and electronparamagnetic resonance (EPR). Kalium iodide (KI), isopropanol (IPA) and benzoquinone (BQ) were used to quench h+, ∙OH, and ∙O2 -, respectively. As shown in Fig. 11 (a), the degradation rate decreased after the use of BQ, KI, and IPA. The order of role of the active radicals can be judged from the degree of decrease in the degradation rate: ∙OH > h+ > ∙O2 -. As shown in Fig. 11 (b), the DMPO/∙O2 - and DMPO/∙OH signals confirmed the production of photoinduced reactive radicals (∙O2 - and ∙OH).

The photocatalytic mechanism of T(I)C15 is illustrated in Fig. 12 . Specifically, photogenerated electron-hole (e-/h+) were formed in both TiO2 and CQDs under light irradiation. As a result of the construction of the S-scheme TiO2@CQDs heterostructure, the e- on the CB of TiO2 transferred to the VB of the CQDs, leaving behind e- (e- on the CB of the CQDs) and h+ (h+ on the VB of the TiO2) with higher redox capacity. The e- on the CB of CQDs can combine with O2 to form ∙O2 - since more negative conduction band potential (ECD) (−1.14 eV vs. NHE) of CQDs than O2/∙O2 - (−0.046 eV vs. NHE) [45]. The h+ on the VB of TiO2 can oxidize H2O or OH– to ∙OH due to the EVB potential of TiO2 (+2.87 eV vs. NHE) is higher than that of H2O/∙OH (+2.27 eV vs. NHE) and OH–/∙OH (+1.99 eV vs. NHE) [46]. The generated ∙O2 -, ∙OH and photogenerated h+ with strong oxidizing ability can react with pollutants to degrade them. In the TiO2(i)CQDs heterostructure, a more effective connected interface between CQDs and TiO2 further facilitated the transfer of e-, which significantly inhibits the photogenerated e-/h+ recombination, thus leading to the formation of more efficient active substances (e-, h+, ∙O2 - and ∙OH) and improving the photocatalytic performance.

The PVDF/TiO2@CQDs hybrid photocatalytic film was prepared using the ink-jet printing technique. Visible light activity of TiO2 was significantly enhanced by constructing S-scheme TiO2@CQDs heterostructures. Compared to TiO2(s)@CQDs (surface composite), TiO2(i)@CQDs (in situ doped) has a smaller particle size (∼10.01 nm), and a narrower size distribution (6∼14 nm), which enhances the photocatalytic effect and reduces the risk of nozzle clogging. The photoactivity was further enhanced by the more efficient charge transport interface established between TiO2 and CQDs in the TiO2(i)@CQDs heterostructure. The optimum printing number was 15 times, in which the catalyst was uniformly, thinly, and densely distributed on the film surface, as well as having the highest photocatalytic performance. The PVDF/TiO2(i)@CQDs film exhibited a high η of 93.71 % of RhB within 150 min under visible light with a catalyst loading of only 0.49 wt%. The ks value of the PVDF/TiO2(i)@CQDs film was more than 4.3 times that of the PVDF/TiO2(s)@CQDs film. The stability of the hybrid film was realized by the Ti-F coordination bond between the catalyst and the film substrate (the η of RhB still reached 88.05 % after 10 consecutive cycles), which avoids high-temperature calcination of the traditional glass carrier in the ink-jet printing process. Polymer-based hybrid photocatalytic films with visible light activity prepared under mild conditions in this study propose a novel strategy for the application of ink-jet printing technology in photocatalysis and the large-area and batch preparation of photocatalytic films.

Zihan Tian: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Shuhao Qin: Formal analysis, Data curation. Jingkui Yang: Investigation, Data curation. Xiao Wu: Investigation, Data curation. Jing Zhang: Visualization, Validation. Jianxin Li: Methodology. Hong Wang: Data curation. Zhenyu Cui: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22178267 and 21978213) and the Innovation Capacity Building Foundation of Scientific Research Institutions in Guizhou Province ([2023]001). We would also like to thank the Analytical & Testing Center of Tiangong University for Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy work.
